summary: Cantrill recounts his first meeting with Andy Bechtolsheim after Sun acquired Kealia, describing an electric encounter between two engineers passionate about their respective crafts. Andy immediately launched into whiteboard diagrams of his hardware designs without introduction, while Cantrill fired back with Solaris 10 features at unprecedented speed. The meeting revealed Andy's rare ability to understand both hardware and software systems holistically, leading to mutual excitement about combining their innovations. This experience explains Cantrill's bullishness on Sun and celebrates the launch of Andy's new machines.
key_points: Andy Bechtolsheim exceeded even his legendary reputation as an engineer
The meeting was characterized by intense, rapid technical exchange
Andy demonstrated unusual cross-domain expertise spanning hardware and software
Their complementary innovations in hardware and software promised unique systems
money_quotes: Here, at long last, was the Real Deal: a fabled engineer who didn't disappoint – a giant who dwarfed the substantial legend that proceeded him.
This might have been one of the few times in my life where I thought I actually needed to speak faster to stay in front of my audience.

Most impressive of all, Andy had a keen intuition for the system – he immediately saw how his innovative hardware and our innovative software could combine to deliver some uniquely innovative systems to our customers.

Not everyone can own a car designed by Enzo Ferrari or a lamp crafted by Louis Comfort Tiffany – but at just over two grand a pop, pretty much everyone can own a machine designed by the greatest single-board computer designer in history.
key_insight: True engineering greatness manifests not just in deep domain expertise but in the ability to see how different pieces of a system can combine to create something greater than the sum of its parts.
